pathophysiology_summary: Obstruction of the cystic duct by a gallstone (the patient has a history of self-limiting biliary colic) causes gallbladder distension, wall oedema and mucosal ischaemia. When obstruction persists beyond ~6 hours, chemical and then bacterial inflammation supervenes — converting intermittent biliary colic into acute cholecystitis. The hallmark shift is that pain becomes CONTINUOUS (>6h) and SYSTEMIC features appear (fever, tachycardia, toxic appearance) — signalling active inflammation/infection rather than transient stone impaction. Untreated, progression is to gallbladder empyema, gangrene, perforation with bile peritonitis, or ascending cholangitis and biliary sepsis. Radiation to the right scapula reflects diaphragmatic/right-phrenic and shared T5-T9 dermatomal referral. Mild scleral icterus raises the possibility of concurrent common-bile-duct stone (choledocholithiasis) or Mirizzi effect and mandates LFTs. Definitive management is source control (cholecystectomy) plus antibiotics; the acute priority is fluid resuscitation, analgesia, antibiotics and surgical assessment.

differential.evidence_basis: Continuous RUQ pain after a fatty meal in someone with prior biliary-type attacks, now with fever and vomiting.
Fits the prior episodes, but biliary colic is self-limiting (<6h) and does NOT cause fever — the continuous pain plus fever argues against it.
Shares a fatty-meal trigger and vomiting; gallstones are the commonest cause. Requires serum lipase to exclude.
Severe upper-abdominal pain with peritonism — but typically generalised board-like rigidity rather than focal RUQ pain radiating to the scapula.
Right-lower-lobe pneumonia can refer pain to the RUQ with fever — needs exclusion with respiratory history/examination.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, cholecystitis should already lead given continuous post-prandial RUQ pain plus fever; the AI should hold pancreatitis, perforation and basal pneumonia as must-not-miss alternatives requiring hospital workup.

differential.evidence_basis: Continuous >6h RUQ pain, fever/chills, bilious vomiting, radiation to right scapula, prior biliary colic — classic acute-cholecystitis constellation.
Scleral icterus + fever + RUQ pain (Charcot's triad) would signal cholangitis, a more dangerous biliary sepsis — LFTs and urgent assessment required.
Excluded by continuous pain >6h and systemic fever — this is no longer self-limiting colic.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The single most important reasoning step is refusing to anchor on the patient's prior benign, self-limiting attacks: the addition of continuous pain and fever escalates this to a surgical abdomen.

differential.evidence_basis: Transabdominal ultrasound expected to show gallbladder wall >3mm, pericholecystic fluid and a positive sonographic Murphy's sign; raised WCC/CRP support infection.
Kept live until serum lipase is confirmed normal.

differential_progression.scoring_note: These investigations are hospital-based; in telehealth the AI cannot obtain them and must escalate to a setting that can.

differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed clinical picture with expected ultrasound and inflammatory markers; requires IV antibiotics, fluids and surgical review.

red_flags.description: Continuous RUQ pain persisting >6 hours WITH new fever/chills — the transition from self-limiting biliary colic to acute cholecystitis (established inflammation/infection)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Marks active gallbladder inflammation, not transient stone impaction; mandates urgent assessment, antibiotics and surgical review.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to empyema, gangrenous cholecystitis, perforation with bile peritonitis, or biliary sepsis.

red_flags.description: Localised RUQ peritonism (guarding/rigidity) and a positive Murphy's sign — localised peritoneal irritation
red_flags.clinical_significance: Indicates transmural inflammation and impending complication; Murphy's sign is a clinician examination finding not obtainable via telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed early perforation; delayed source control.

red_flags.description: Mild scleral icterus with fever and RUQ pain — possible common-bile-duct stone / ascending cholangitis (Charcot's triad component)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Raises the more dangerous possibility of biliary sepsis/cholangitis requiring urgent biliary drainage; jaundice is subtle and clinician-observed.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed cholangitis progressing to septic shock.

red_flags.description: Systemic toxicity — fever 38.6, tachycardia, toxic/flushed appearance, plus dehydration from intractable vomiting
red_flags.clinical_significance: Early biliary sepsis and volume depletion; needs IV fluids and antibiotics and time-critical assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to sepsis, hypovolaemia and organ dysfunction.

red_flags.description: Sudden complete resolution of pain followed by lethargy and generalised abdominal distension — gallbladder perforation with generalised bile peritonitis
red_flags.clinical_significance: A deceptive pain-free interval after perforation precedes rapid deterioration into peritonitis and shock.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Fatal peritonitis/septic shock if not treated as an emergency.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating this as 'just another gallstone attack' that will pass, because previous episodes self-resolved — missing that continuous pain plus fever now signals infection.
Offering home analgesia/antiemetics and a routine GP follow-up instead of arranging same-day ED assessment for imaging, IV antibiotics and surgical review.
Overlooking the scleral icterus and failing to consider ascending cholangitis (Charcot's triad), which is more dangerous and needs urgent biliary drainage.
Failing to keep pancreatitis live and ensure serum lipase is checked, since gallstones commonly cause both.
Attempting to confirm the diagnosis remotely (there is no way to auscultate, palpate, obtain a Murphy's sign, take bloods or perform ultrasound over chat) instead of escalating to a setting that can.

risk_scores.tool_name: Tokyo Guidelines (TG18) severity grade — clinical estimate
risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Grade I–II (mild-to-moderate acute cholecystitis); watch for progression to Grade III (organ dysfunction)
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Even Grade I mandates admission, IV antibiotics and surgical review; systemic features here push toward Grade II.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Murphy's sign (requires deep subcostal palpation during inspiration)
Abdominal guarding, rigidity and localised peritonism on palpation
Objective temperature, heart rate, blood pressure and respiratory rate (no connected device)
Scleral icterus (a clinician-observed sign; not reliably self-reported)
Full blood count, CRP, liver function tests and serum lipase
Transabdominal ultrasound (gallbladder wall thickness, pericholecystic fluid, sonographic Murphy's sign)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Arrange urgent same-day Emergency Department assessment (T4) by private transport with the sibling; advise strict nil-by-mouth; do not attempt to confirm or treat remotely. Escalate to 000 (T5) if signs of perforation, sepsis or shock develop.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The diagnosis is strongly suggested clinically, but telehealth cannot confirm it or exclude cholangitis/pancreatitis/perforation — the AI must present it as a suspected surgical abdomen needing hospital workup, not a confirmed benign gallstone attack.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Diagnosis rests on local inflammation (Murphy's sign / RUQ pain), systemic inflammation (fever, raised WCC/CRP) and imaging; management is antibiotics, supportive care and early cholecystectomy where appropriate.

guidelines_referenced.reference: eTG (Therapeutic Guidelines, Australia) — biliary tract infections
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Empirical IV antibiotic therapy directed at enteric Gram-negative organisms and anaerobes, initiated in hospital, alongside fluid resuscitation and surgical assessment.
